judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the exact vulnerability fixed by the commit: a missing bounds check (p + 4 > max) before two pj_memcpy calls in parse_query(). While the finding uses CWE-125 (OOB read) and the CVE uses CWE-787 (OOB write/buffer overflow), both describe the same underlying issue. The fix in the diff is precisely the addition of this bounds check.

actual_issue_summary: Buffer overflow in PJSIP DNS packet parsing. In parse_query(), after parsing the DNS name, the code reads type (2 bytes) and class (2 bytes) via pj_memcpy without checking that 4 bytes remain in the buffer. The fix adds a bounds check (p + 4 > max) before reading these fields. The commit also fixes related issues in get_name_len() and get_name() where label length validation was insufficient.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions several TensorFlow operations are missing validation for the shapes of the tensor arguments involved in the call. Depending on the API, this can result in undefined behavior and segfault or `CHECK`-fail related crashes but in some scenarios writes and reads from heap populated arrays are also possible. We have discovered these issues internally via tooling while working on improving/testing GPU op determinism. As such, we don't have reproducers and there will be multiple fixes for these issues. These fixes will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick these commits on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: Out-of-bounds read/write on transposed_rhs_reshaped when input and rhs have different batch dimensions. The code iterates up to batch_size (derived from input's batch dimensions) but accesses transposed_rhs_reshaped which has a potentially smaller batch dimension derived from rhs. No validation ensures batch dimensions match.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds memory access leading to denial of service (crash) or potential information disclosure/code execution. An attacker can craft inputs where input has larger batch dimensions than rhs, causing reads/writes past the transposed_rhs buffer.

state.value: derived from input's batch dimensions, potentially larger than rhs's batch dimension
derived from rhs's batch dimensions, potentially smaller than batch_size

initial_state.value: attacker-controlled tensor with shape e.g. [2, 3, 1], same rank and matching last two dims but different batch dims

step_by_step_execution.description: ndims, n, nrhs are computed from input and rhs shapes

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: ndims is 3
Both have rank 3
input is square: dim_size(1) == dim_size(2) == 3
rhs.dim_size(1) == 3 == n

step_by_step_execution.description: No check on batch dimensions (outer dimensions) — the missing validation. Execution proceeds past all checks.
input_copy_reshaped is created from input, batch_size derived from it
transposed_rhs is allocated with shape derived from rhs, then reshaped

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: batch_size is 4 so loop runs for batch=0,1,2,3, but transposed_rhs_reshaped only has 2 entries in batch dimension

step_by_step_execution.description: When batch >= 2, accessing transposed_rhs_reshaped(batch, 0, 0) goes out of bounds, causing OOB read/write

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the missing batch dimension validation between input and rhs tensors. The fix adds exactly this check (a loop verifying input.dim_size(dim) == rhs.dim_size(dim) for all outer dimensions). The consequence described — OOB access when iterating with batch_size derived from input over a smaller rhs buffer — matches the CVE's description of missing shape validation leading to crashes or heap read/writes.

actual_issue_summary: The MatrixSolve GPU op did not validate that the batch (outer) dimensions of the input matrix and right-hand side tensor match. When they differ, batch_size is derived from input's shape and used to index into rhs-derived buffers, causing out-of-bounds memory access, crashes, or silent data corruption. The fix adds a loop checking that all outer dimensions of input and rhs are equal.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.StagePeek` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code assumes `index` is a scalar but there is no validation for this before accessing its value. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

undesired_operation.description: A negative int from user input is implicitly converted to a very large std::size_t value when assigned to 'index'. This large value is then passed to buf->Peek(), potentially causing an out-of-bounds read or other undefined behavior.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds access: a negative input value (e.g., -1) becomes a massive index (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF on 64-bit), potentially leading to information disclosure, crash, or memory corruption depending on how buf->Peek handles the index.

state.value: very large value due to signed-to-unsigned conversion of negative int

initial_state.value: attacker-controlled tensor with negative scalar int value

step_by_step_execution.description: The scalar value is read from input tensor and cast to std::size_t
buf->Peek is called with the massive index value

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is specifically about missing validation that the `index` input tensor is a scalar. When a non-scalar tensor is passed, `.scalar<int>()()` triggers a CHECK-failure causing denial of service. The finding instead describes a signed-to-unsigned integer conversion issue with negative values, which is a different problem from the one the CVE addresses. The fix removes the index handling entirely rather than adding a negativity check.

actual_issue_summary: The implementation of tf.raw_ops.StagePeek assumes the `index` input is a scalar tensor without validation. When a non-scalar tensor is provided, calling `.scalar<int>()()` triggers a CHECK-failure, causing a denial of service crash. The fix removes the index parameter handling entirely, replacing `buf->Peek(index, &tuple)` with `buf->Get(&tuple)`.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the shape inference function for `Transpose` is vulnerable to a heap buffer overflow. This occurs whenever `perm` contains negative elements. The shape inference function does not validate that the indices in `perm` are all valid. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: A negative value in the perm tensor passes the bounds check (in_idx >= rank) and is used as an index to c->Dim(input, in_idx), resulting in an out-of-bounds read/access with a negative index.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read via negative index. An attacker providing a crafted perm tensor with negative values can read memory before the shape dimensions array, potentially causing a crash (denial of service) or information disclosure.

step_by_step_execution.description: rank is determined from input shape or perm tensor
perm data is read into data vector
in_idx is assigned from data[i] in loop iteration

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: in_idx is -5, rank is 3, so -5 >= 3 is false; the check does not catch negative values

step_by_step_execution.description: c->Dim(input, in_idx) is called with negative in_idx, causing out-of-bounds access

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the CVE: negative perm values bypass the bounds check (only checks >= rank, not negative) and cause an out-of-bounds access in c->Dim(input, in_idx). The fix adds the check `in_idx <= -rank` to catch negative values. The CWE differs (CWE-125 vs CWE-703) but the underlying vulnerability is the same.

actual_issue_summary: The shape inference function for Transpose only checked if perm indices were >= rank but did not check for negative values that were out of range (< -rank). This allowed negative perm values to cause a heap buffer overflow/out-of-bounds access when used as indices in c->Dim(input, in_idx). The fix adds the check `in_idx <= -rank` to reject such values.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of SVDF in TFLite is [vulnerable to a null pointer error](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/svdf.cc#L300-L313). The [`GetVariableInput` function](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L115-L119) can return a null pointer but `GetTensorData` assumes that the argument is always a valid tensor. Furthermore, because `GetVariableInput` calls [`GetMutableInput`](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L82-L90) which might return `nullptr`, the `tensor->is_variable` expression can also trigger a null pointer exception. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5b048e87e4e55990dae6b547add4dae59f4e1c76. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: tensor is dereferenced (tensor->is_variable) without a null check after GetMutableInput, which can return nullptr. This causes a null pointer dereference.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash (segmentation fault) when GetMutableInput returns nullptr due to an invalid index or other error condition.

step_by_step_execution.description: GetMutableInput is called and may return nullptr

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: There is no null check in the vulnerable version, so execution proceeds directly to the dereference

step_by_step_execution.description: tensor->is_variable is evaluated, dereferencing a null pointer

out_state.value: crash / undefined behavior due to null pointer dereference

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the missing null check on the return value of GetMutableInput before dereferencing tensor->is_variable. This is exactly the vulnerability described in CVE-2021-37681 and fixed by the commit. The CWE-476 (NULL Pointer Dereference) is consistent with the CVE's CWE-369, as the NVD description explicitly states this is a null pointer error.

actual_issue_summary: GetVariableInput dereferences the pointer returned by GetMutableInput (tensor->is_variable) without checking for nullptr. GetMutableInput can return nullptr, leading to a null pointer dereference. The fix adds a null check before the dereference.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions during TensorFlow's Grappler optimizer phase, constant folding might attempt to deep copy a resource tensor. This results in a segfault, as these tensors are supposed to not change. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: Integer overflow in the multiplication `out_shape.num_elements() * DataTypeSize(n->output_type(i))` can wrap around to a small value (e.g., 0), bypassing the size check against max_constant_size_in_bytes.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via excessive memory allocation or out-of-memory crash.

initial_state.value: a non-constant node with large output shape

step_by_step_execution.description: Multiplication of num_elements and DataTypeSize overflows

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Due to overflow the product is small

judgments.reasoning: The finding describes a hypothetical integer overflow in the size check, which is unrelated to CVE-2021-41204. The actual CVE is about constant folding attempting to deep copy DT_RESOURCE tensors (which cannot be deep copied), causing a segfault. The fix adds a check to exclude DT_RESOURCE constants from constant folding. The finding identifies a completely different issue.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is that IsConstantFoldable unconditionally returns true for constant nodes, including DT_RESOURCE type constants. Resource tensors cannot be deep copied, so when the Grappler optimizer's constant folding phase attempts to deep copy them, it causes a segfault. The fix adds a check to return false for DT_RESOURCE constants.

cve_desc: In Das U-Boot through 2022.07-rc5, an integer signedness error and resultant stack-based buffer overflow in the "i2c md" command enables the corruption of the return address pointer of the do_i2c_md function.

undesired_operation.description: When 'length' (uint) exceeds INT_MAX, assigning it to 'nbytes' (int) causes nbytes to become negative. In the do-while loop (which always executes at least once), the negative nbytes is compared against DISP_LINE_LEN and selected as linebytes. This negative linebytes is then passed as the size argument to dm_i2c_read/i2c_read, which if interpreted as an unsigned/size_t parameter, becomes a very large value, causing a massive write into the stack-allocated linebuf[DISP_LINE_LEN] buffer.
code_snippets: ret = i2c_read(chip, addr, alen, linebuf, linebytes);
undesired_operation.impact: Stack buffer overflow — attacker-controlled length from command line argument causes a massive I2C read into a small stack buffer, potentially leading to code execution or crash.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly describes CVE-2022-34835: the integer signedness error where unsigned 'length' (e.g. 0x80000100) is assigned to signed 'nbytes', making it negative, bypassing the DISP_LINE_LEN comparison, and resulting in a stack buffer overflow via dm_i2c_read/i2c_read. The CWEs (CWE-120 buffer overflow, CWE-681 integer signedness) align with the CVE's CWE-787 (out-of-bounds write).

actual_issue_summary: Integer signedness error in do_i2c_md: 'nbytes' and 'linebytes' are declared as signed int but receive values from unsigned 'length'. When length >= 0x80000000, nbytes becomes negative, causing the comparison 'nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN' to be false, so linebytes gets the large negative value instead of 16. This is passed as the size to dm_i2c_read/i2c_read, overflowing the 16-byte stack buffer linebuf. Fix: change nbytes, linebytes, j, and alen to unsigned int.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of sparse reduction operations in TensorFlow can trigger accesses outside of bounds of heap allocated data. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/a1bc56203f21a5a4995311825ffaba7a670d7747/tensorflow/core/kernels/sparse_reduce_op.cc#L217-L228) fails to validate that each reduction group does not overflow and that each corresponding index does not point to outside the bounds of the input tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 87158f43f05f2720a374f3e6d22a7aaa3a33f750. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: The computed flat index 'idx' from CoordinatesToFlatIndex is used to write into out_flat without any bounds check. An attacker who controls the sparse tensor's indices can cause g.group() to return coordinates that, when multiplied by output_strides, produce an out-of-bounds index into the output tensor.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds write into the output tensor buffer. An attacker can craft sparse tensor indices to write to arbitrary memory relative to the output buffer, potentially achieving code execution or denial of service.

state.value: Can be negative or >= out_flat.size() due to attacker-controlled coordinates

initial_state.value: Allocated output tensor with size based on reduction.reduced_shape

step_by_step_execution.description: SparseTensor is created from attacker-controlled indices and values
Groups are iterated and CoordinatesToFlatIndex computes idx from g.group() coordinates and output_strides

out_state.value: Can be any int64 value, including negative or >= out_flat.size()

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: No such check exists in the vulnerable code. The index is used directly without validation, so out-of-bounds values pass through unchecked.

step_by_step_execution.description: out_flat(idx) = reduced_val() writes to an unchecked index

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly matches CVE-2021-37635. The fix adds exactly the bounds check on idx (idx >= 0 && idx < out_flat.size()) that the finding identifies as missing. The CWE differs slightly (CWE-787 write vs CWE-125 read in NVD) but the underlying issue is the same missing bounds validation on the computed index.

actual_issue_summary: The sparse reduction operation computes a flat index from group coordinates without validating that the index falls within the bounds of the output tensor. The fix adds two checks: (1) that group size matches output_strides size, and (2) that the computed index is within [0, out_flat.size()). Without these checks, attacker-controlled sparse tensor indices can cause out-of-bounds heap access.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. An attacker can cause a denial of service by controlling the values of `num_segments` tensor argument for `UnsortedSegmentJoin`. This is because the implementation(https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/a2a607db15c7cd01d754d37e5448d72a13491bdb/tensorflow/core/kernels/unsorted_segment_join_op.cc#L92-L93) assumes that the `num_segments` tensor is a valid scalar. Since the tensor is empty the `CHECK` involved in `.scalar<T>()()` that checks that the number of elements is exactly 1 will be invalidated and this would result in process termination. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.5.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.4.2, TensorFlow 2.3.3, TensorFlow 2.2.3 and TensorFlow 2.1.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: num_segments is read from attacker-controlled input tensor and used directly in GetOutputShape without validation that it is non-negative. A negative num_segments value can cause integer overflow or underflow in TensorShape computation, leading to an incorrectly sized output tensor allocation, crash, or undefined behavior.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or potentially memory corruption — attacker provides a negative num_segments value causing invalid tensor shape computation and allocation

step_by_step_execution.description: num_segments is read from the scalar tensor
num_segments is used in GetOutputShape to compute output tensor shape

out_state.value: potentially invalid shape due to negative dimension

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2021-29552) is about providing an empty num_segments tensor (with 0 elements), which causes a CHECK failure in .scalar<T>()() because that method asserts exactly 1 element exists. The fix adds a check that NumElements() != 0 before calling .scalar(). The finding instead describes a different hypothetical issue about negative num_segments values, which is not what this CVE addresses.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is that UnsortedSegmentJoin calls .scalar<T>()() on the num_segments tensor without first verifying it has exactly one element. An attacker can pass an empty tensor, causing the internal CHECK assertion in .scalar() to fail, resulting in process termination (denial of service). The fix adds an OP_REQUIRES check that num_segments_tensor.NumElements() != 0 before the .scalar() call.

cve_desc: A memory leak in the rsi_send_beacon() function in drivers/net/wireless/rsi/rsi_91x_mgmt.c in the Linux kernel through 5.3.11 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering rsi_prepare_beacon() failures, aka CID-d563131ef23c.

undesired_operation.description: When rsi_prepare_beacon() fails, the function returns -EINVAL without freeing the allocated skb, causing a memory leak.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via memory exhaustion — an attacker who can repeatedly trigger beacon preparation failures can leak kernel memory (each leak is up to MAX_MGMT_PKT_SIZE bytes).

state.value: allocated but not freed before return

step_by_step_execution.description: skb is allocated via dev_alloc_skb(MAX_MGMT_PKT_SIZE)

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Allocation succeeded, so skb is non-NULL

step_by_step_execution.description: rsi_prepare_beacon(common, skb) is called and fails (returns non-zero)

out_state.value: valid pointer to allocated sk_buff (still allocated)

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: rsi_prepare_beacon fails, entering the error path

step_by_step_execution.description: Function returns -EINVAL without calling dev_kfree_skb(skb), leaking the skb

out_state.value: leaked — no reference retained and no free performed

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2019-19071: a memory leak in rsi_send_beacon when rsi_prepare_beacon fails, due to missing dev_kfree_skb(skb) before returning -EINVAL. The CWE, impact, and code location all match.

actual_issue_summary: In rsi_send_beacon(), when rsi_prepare_beacon() fails, the function returns -EINVAL without freeing the previously allocated skb, causing a memory leak (CWE-401). The fix adds dev_kfree_skb(skb) before the return.

cve_desc: common/password.c in Pengutronix barebox through 2021.07.0 leaks timing information because strncmp is used during hash comparison.

undesired_operation.description: strncmp is used to compare binary hash/key data. strncmp stops at the first null byte ('\0'), so if both the computed hash (passwd1_sum) and the stored hash (passwd2_sum or key) share a common prefix up to an embedded null byte, they will be considered equal regardless of remaining bytes. An attacker needs only to find a password whose hash starts with '\0' (~1/256 probability per attempt) to bypass authentication.
undesired_operation.impact: Authentication bypass — an attacker can gain access by supplying a password whose hash begins with a null byte, which strncmp will treat as matching any stored hash that also begins with a null byte (or has matching prefix up to the first null byte). This dramatically reduces the effective hash comparison length.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2021-37848) is specifically about timing side-channel information leakage because strncmp is used for hash comparison, allowing an attacker to deduce hash contents by observing comparison timing. The finding instead describes a null-byte truncation issue (strncmp treating null bytes as string terminators in binary data). While both issues stem from the same misuse of strncmp and are fixed by the same patch (switching to crypto_memneq), the finding describes a fundamentally different attack vector (authentication bypass via null-byte collisions) rather than the timing side-channel described by the CVE (CWE-200).

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is a timing side-channel in password hash comparison. strncmp was used to compare password hashes, which is not constant-time — it returns early on the first differing byte. An attacker can observe timing differences to progressively learn the stored password hash. The fix replaces strncmp with crypto_memneq, which performs constant-time comparison.

cve_desc: A memory leak in the fastrpc_dma_buf_attach() function in drivers/misc/fastrpc.c in the Linux kernel before 5.3.9 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering dma_get_sgtable() failures, aka CID-fc739a058d99.

undesired_operation.description: When dma_get_sgtable() fails, the function returns -EINVAL without freeing the previously allocated 'a', leaking kernel heap memory.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via kernel memory exhaustion — an attacker who can repeatedly trigger the dma_get_sgtable failure path can leak kernel heap memory on each call.

step_by_step_execution.description: 'a' is allocated via kzalloc

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: kzalloc succeeded, so a is non-NULL

step_by_step_execution.description: dma_get_sgtable is called and fails, returning a negative value

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: dma_get_sgtable failed

step_by_step_execution.description: Function returns -EINVAL without calling kfree(a), leaking the allocation

out_state.value: leaked — no reference retained, never freed

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2019-19069: a memory leak of 'a' when dma_get_sgtable() fails in fastrpc_dma_buf_attach(). The fix adds kfree(a) before the return.

actual_issue_summary: Memory leak in fastrpc_dma_buf_attach(): when dma_get_sgtable() fails, the function returns without freeing the kzalloc'd 'a' structure. The fix adds kfree(a) before the error return.

cve_desc: In drivers/hid/hid-elo.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.11, a memory leak exists for a certain hid_parse error condition.

undesired_operation.description: When hid_parse() or hid_hw_start() fails, the error path 'err_free' calls kfree(priv) but does not call usb_put_dev(priv->usbdev) to release the USB device reference obtained by usb_get_dev(). This leaks a reference count on the USB device, preventing it from ever being freed.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service — an attacker who can repeatedly trigger probe failures (e.g., by causing hid_parse or hid_hw_start to fail) can leak USB device references and associated memory, eventually exhausting kernel memory.

state.value: valid USB device pointer with incremented refcount from usb_get_dev()

step_by_step_execution.description: priv is allocated via kzalloc

out_state.value: valid pointer to allocated elo_priv struct

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Allocation succeeded

step_by_step_execution.description: usb_get_dev increments refcount on the USB device and stores it in priv->usbdev

out_state.value: valid USB device pointer, refcount incremented

step_by_step_execution.description: hid_parse(hdev) is called and returns an error

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: hid_parse returned an error code

step_by_step_execution.description: Execution jumps to err_free label, which calls kfree(priv) but NOT usb_put_dev()

in_state.value: valid USB device pointer with incremented refcount

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2022-27950: the missing usb_put_dev() call in the error path when hid_parse() fails causes a memory/reference leak. The fix adds usb_put_dev(udev) in the err_free path, which is precisely what this finding identifies.

actual_issue_summary: In elo_probe(), when hid_parse() or hid_hw_start() fails, the error handling path frees priv but does not call usb_put_dev() to release the USB device reference acquired by usb_get_dev(), causing a reference count leak. The fix adds usb_put_dev(udev) to the err_free error path.

cve_desc: Integer overflow in the VNC display driver in QEMU before 2.1.0 allows attachers to cause a denial of service (process crash) via a CLIENT_CUT_TEXT message, which triggers an infinite loop.

undesired_operation.description: Integer overflow in '8 + dlen' where dlen is a uint32_t read from attacker-controlled data. When dlen is large (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF), '8 + dlen' wraps around to a small value (e.g., 7). The function returns this small value, causing the caller to invoke the function again with insufficient data. On the second call, len is not 1 and not 8, so both guards are skipped, and client_cut_text is called with the attacker-controlled large length and a data pointer (data + 8) that extends beyond the actual buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: An attacker can cause an out-of-bounds memory access by sending a crafted VNC_MSG_CLIENT_CUT_TEXT message with a large dlen value, potentially leading to information disclosure or denial of service (crash).

state.value: wraps around to small value due to integer overflow, e.g. 7

initial_state.value: unset, will be read from data[4..7]

step_by_step_execution.description: Switch on data[0] matches VNC_MSG_CLIENT_CUT_TEXT case

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: len is 8, not 1
len is 8

step_by_step_execution.description: dlen is read from attacker-controlled data at offset 4

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: 0xFFFFFFFF > 0

step_by_step_execution.description: return 8 + dlen overflows: 8 + 0xFFFFFFFF = 7 (wraps around in int)

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the integer overflow in '8 + dlen' in the CLIENT_CUT_TEXT handler, which is the exact root cause of CVE-2015-5239. The CVE describes this as causing an infinite loop / denial of service. The finding describes a slightly different consequence (OOB access) but the root cause — unchecked large dlen leading to integer overflow in 8 + dlen — is exactly the vulnerability fixed by adding the dlen > (1 << 20) check.

actual_issue_summary: Integer overflow in the VNC CLIENT_CUT_TEXT message handler: a malicious client can send a very large dlen value (up to 2^32-1), causing '8 + dlen' to overflow and wrap around. This leads to an infinite loop or other misbehavior resulting in denial of service. The fix adds a 1MB limit on the payload size.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. Versions 2.12 and prior contain a stack buffer overflow vulnerability that affects PJSUA2 users or users that call the API `pjmedia_sdp_print(), pjmedia_sdp_media_print()`. Applications that do not use PJSUA2 and do not directly call `pjmedia_sdp_print()` or `pjmedia_sdp_media_print()` should not be affected. A patch is available on the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. There are currently no known workarounds.

undesired_operation.description: The initial buffer length check does not account for the space and data written by the fmt_count loop. Each iteration writes a space character plus fmt[i].slen bytes, but none of this is included in the length check. With sufficient fmt entries, writes via pj_memcpy and *p++ will go past the end of the buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap or stack buffer overflow — an attacker who controls the SDP media description (e.g. via a crafted SIP message) can write arbitrary data past the buffer boundary, potentially achieving remote code execution or denial of service.

state.value: past end of buf due to unchecked writes in fmt loop
large enough that cumulative fmt sizes exceed remaining buffer space

m= line, media, port, transport are written to buffer, advancing p

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Attacker controls fmt_count; loop iterates for each format entry

out_state.value: past end of buffer after sufficient iterations

step_by_step_execution.description: pj_memcpy writes fmt data beyond buffer boundary

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2022-24764: a stack buffer overflow in print_media_desc due to missing bounds checks in the fmt loop and before writing \r\n. The fix adds precisely those bounds checks.

actual_issue_summary: Stack buffer overflow in print_media_desc(): the loop writing format entries (fmt) and the subsequent \r\n write lack bounds checking against the remaining buffer space. An attacker with many or large fmt entries can overflow the buffer. The fix adds bounds checks before each fmt write and before writing \r\n.

cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2.

undesired_operation.description: Integer overflow in 'len+2' passed to mrb_stack_extend causes insufficient stack allocation, then the while loop writes 'len' mrb_value elements past the allocated stack buffer.
code_snippets: mrb_stack_extend(mrb, len+2); /* for receiver and (optional) block */
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow — attacker can overwrite heap memory beyond the fiber's stack, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution or crash.

state.value: very large value causing len+2 to overflow

initial_state.value: attacker-controlled number of arguments passed to fiber
pointer to array of mrb_value arguments

step_by_step_execution.description: mrb_stack_extend is called with len+2 which overflows

out_state.value: small due to overflow in len+2

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop copies len elements starting at stbase+1

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is a NULL Pointer Dereference (CWE-703). The actual issue is that when 15 or more arguments are passed to a fiber, `cibase->n` is set to a value >= 15, which is a sentinel in mruby's VM indicating arguments are packed in an array. But the vulnerable code doesn't pack them into an array, so the VM later tries to access a non-existent array object, dereferencing a NULL pointer. The finding incorrectly describes an integer overflow / heap buffer overflow scenario, which is not the vulnerability fixed by this commit.

actual_issue_summary: When a fiber is resumed/transferred with 15 or more arguments, mruby's VM convention requires that arguments be packed into an array (signaled by n=15). The vulnerable code set cibase->n to the raw len value without packing arguments into an array when len >= 15, causing the VM to later attempt to unpack a non-existent array, resulting in a NULL pointer dereference. The fix packs 15+ arguments into an array and sets len to 15 as the sentinel value.

cve_desc: Use after free in utf_ptr2char in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4646.

undesired_operation.description: In the MULTIBYTECODE case, `rex.input += len` is executed unconditionally after both the composing and non-composing match paths, even when status is RA_NOMATCH. The `len` value comes from the pattern operand's multibyte character length (`mb_ptr2len(opnd)`), not from the input. When the input at `rex.input` has fewer valid bytes than `len` (e.g., `*rex.input == NUL` at end of line), this advances `rex.input` past the NUL terminator, enabling out-of-bounds reads on subsequent accesses to `rex.input`.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read past NUL terminator of the input line buffer. An attacker who can control the regex pattern (containing a multibyte character) and the input string can trigger reads of heap/buffer memory beyond the input string, potentially causing information disclosure or a crash (denial of service).

state.value: points at or near NUL terminator

step_by_step_execution.description: op is determined to be MULTIBYTECODE
len is set from mb_ptr2len of the pattern operand

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The pattern contains a valid multibyte character so its length is at least 2

step_by_step_execution.description: Non-composing path: comparison fails, status set to RA_NOMATCH, but execution falls through to rex.input += len

judgments.reasoning: The finding describes a potential out-of-bounds read in the MULTIBYTECODE case. The actual CVE-2022-1154 is a use-after-free in the RE_MARK case, where `getmark_buf()` can free the buffer line, and `rex.line`/`rex.input` become dangling pointers. The fix re-fetches the line after calling `getmark_buf()`. The finding describes a completely different issue unrelated to the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: In the RE_MARK case of the old regexp engine, calling `getmark_buf()` can cause the buffer line pointed to by `rex.line` and `rex.input` to be freed. After `getmark_buf()` returns, `rex.line` and `rex.input` are stale pointers to freed memory, resulting in a use-after-free when they are subsequently accessed. The fix saves the column offset before calling `getmark_buf()`, then re-fetches the line via `reg_getline()` and recalculates `rex.input` afterward.

cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in r_bin_java_bootstrap_methods_attr_new function in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.7.0. The bug causes the program reads data past the end 2f the intented buffer. Typically, this can allow attackers to read sensitive information from other memory locations or cause a crash. More details see [CWE-125: Out-of-bounds read](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/125.html).

undesired_operation.description: R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT reads 2 bytes from buffer at offset 6 (bytes 6-7) without checking that sz >= 8, causing an out-of-bounds read when sz < 8
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure or crash — attacker provides a crafted Java class file with a small attribute body, triggering a heap/stack out-of-bounds read

step_by_step_execution.description: offset is incremented by 6

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: r_bin_java_default_attr_new succeeds and returns a valid pointer
No bounds check exists in the vulnerable code, so execution proceeds directly to the R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT call

step_by_step_execution.description: R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT reads 2 bytes from buffer at offset 6, but sz < 8, so the read goes past the buffer boundary

judgments.reasoning: The finding precisely identifies the out-of-bounds read that the fix addresses: reading R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT at offset 6 without verifying sz >= 8. The fix adds exactly this check (offset + 8 > sz). This matches CVE-2022-1452 (CWE-125 OOB read in r_bin_java_bootstrap_methods_attr_new).

actual_issue_summary: In r_bin_java_bootstrap_methods_attr_new, R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT is called at offset 6 without verifying that the buffer is large enough (sz >= offset+8), leading to an out-of-bounds read. The fix adds a bounds check `if (offset + 8 > sz)` before the read.

cve_desc: QEMU, possibly before 2.0.0, allows local users to cause a denial of service (divide-by-zero error and crash) via a zero value in the (1) tracks field to the seek_to_sector function in block/parallels.c or (2) extent_size field in the bochs function in block/bochs.c.

undesired_operation.description: If s->catalog_size is a signed int type, attacker-controlled catalog_entries value >= 0x80000000 from the disk image is converted to a negative int, bypassing the 'catalog_size > INT_MAX / 4' check. Then 's->catalog_size * 4' produces a negative value which, when implicitly converted to size_t for g_malloc, becomes an enormous allocation size (e.g., ~18 EiB on 64-bit), causing g_malloc to abort and crash the process (DoS). Even if the allocation somehow succeeds, bdrv_pread would read a wrong amount of data causing heap buffer overflow.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash from g_malloc abort on impossibly large allocation, or potential heap buffer overflow if allocation size wraps to a small value on 32-bit systems

step_by_step_execution.description: catalog_size is read from disk image header

out_state.value: negative value if stored as signed int (e.g. -2147483648)

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Negative value is not greater than INT_MAX/4, so check is bypassed

step_by_step_execution.description: g_malloc called with s->catalog_size * 4, negative int converted to huge size_t

out_state.value: enormous value after implicit conversion to size_t

judgments.reasoning: CVE-2014-0142 is specifically about a divide-by-zero caused by s->tracks being zero, which is used as a divisor in seek_to_sector. The fix adds a check for s->tracks == 0. This finding describes a completely different issue related to integer overflow in catalog_size, which is unrelated to the divide-by-zero vulnerability that the CVE addresses.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is a divide-by-zero in QEMU's parallels block driver. The s->tracks field is read from a disk image header without validation. When s->tracks is zero, it is later used as a divisor in seek_to_sector(), causing a crash. The fix adds a check rejecting images where s->tracks == 0.

cve_desc: Integer overflow in the ljpeg_start function in dcraw 7.00 and earlier allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted image, which triggers a buffer overflow, related to the len variable.

undesired_operation.description: fread is called with a negative 'len' value that gets implicitly cast to a very large size_t, attempting to write far more than 0x10000 bytes into the stack-allocated 'data' buffer, causing a stack buffer overflow.
undesired_operation.impact: Remote code execution or crash — attacker controls the JPEG marker length field in the input file, which determines the 'len' value passed to fread.

step_by_step_execution.description: fread reads 4 bytes into data: 2 bytes for tag and 2 bytes for len field
len is computed as (data[2] << 8 | data[3]) - 2 = (0 | 0) - 2 = -2

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Attacker provides a valid marker tag > 0xff00

step_by_step_execution.description: fread(data, 1, len, ifp) is called; len=-2 is implicitly cast to size_t, becoming a very large value (e.g. 0xFFFFFFFE on 32-bit), attempting to read/write far beyond the 0x10000-byte data buffer

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly identifies the CVE-2015-3885 vulnerability: the integer overflow/sign issue in 'len' (declared as int) that allows a negative value to be passed to fread, causing a buffer overflow. The fix changes len to ushort to prevent negative values.

undesired_operation.description: In the 0xffda case, data[0] is attacker-controlled and used to compute array indices 1+data[0]*2 and 3+data[0]*2 into the 'data' buffer. These indices can reference bytes beyond the 'len' bytes actually read by fread, accessing uninitialized stack memory.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure — uninitialized stack data is read and stored in jhead structure fields, potentially leaking sensitive information from the stack.

state.value: up to 511, beyond len bytes read
stack buffer, partially initialized up to len bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: len is computed from marker length field
fread reads len bytes into data
In 0xffda case, data[0] is used to index into data at 1+data[0]*2 and 3+data[0]*2

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a potential out-of-bounds read in the 0xffda handler, which is a separate issue from CVE-2015-3885. The CVE is specifically about the integer overflow in the 'len' variable (int vs ushort) leading to a buffer overflow via fread. The fix only changes len's type and does not address the 0xffda indexing.

actual_issue_summary: The 'len' variable in ljpeg_start() was declared as 'int', allowing the expression (data[2] << 8 | data[3]) - 2 to produce a negative value (e.g., when the length field is 0 or 1). This negative int, when passed to fread() as a size_t parameter, wraps to a very large value, causing a stack buffer overflow. The fix changes len from 'int' to 'ushort', ensuring it stays non-negative.

cve_desc: In Pure-FTPd before 1.0.50, an incorrect max_filesize quota mechanism in the server allows attackers to upload files of unbounded size, which may lead to denial of service or a server hang. This occurs because a certain greater-than-zero test does not anticipate an initial -1 value. (Versions 1.0.23 through 1.0.49 are affected.)

undesired_operation.description: When strlen(name) equals sizeof(shm_data_cur->filename), the expression 'sl - sizeof shm_data_cur->filename - 1U' underflows to SIZE_MAX causing out-of-bounds read
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read leading to crash or information disclosure

initial_state.value: strlen(name), potentially equal to sizeof shm_data_cur->filename

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: When sl equals sizeof shm_data_cur->filename, the condition is false so the else branch is taken

step_by_step_execution.description: Computes index sl - sizeof shm_data_cur->filename - 1U which underflows

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a potential integer underflow in the FTPWHO shared memory filename copying code. The actual CVE-2021-40524 is about an incorrect max_filesize quota mechanism where the condition `max_filesize >= (off_t) 0` prevents `max_filesize` from being computed when it starts at -1, allowing unbounded file uploads. The finding is completely unrelated to the quota bypass vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: In the QUOTAS check, the condition `max_filesize >= (off_t) 0` short-circuits the evaluation because max_filesize is initialized to -1. This means `max_filesize = user_quota_size - quota.size` is never computed, so max_filesize remains -1 (interpreted as unlimited) when passed to ul_init(), allowing files of unbounded size to be uploaded, bypassing quota enforcement. The fix removes the `max_filesize >= (off_t) 0` guard so max_filesize is always computed from the quota.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming RTCP BYE message contains a reason's length, this declared length is not checked against the actual received packet size, potentially resulting in an out-of-bound read access. This issue affects all users that use PJMEDIA and RTCP. A malicious actor can send a RTCP BYE message with an invalid reason length. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

undesired_operation.description: pj_memcpy reads reason.slen bytes from pkt+9, but reason.slen is derived from a length byte inside the packet and only clamped to the destination buffer size, NOT to the remaining packet size (size - 9). An attacker can send a small packet (e.g. size=10) with a large reason-length byte (e.g. 200), causing a read of up to sizeof(peer_sdes_buf_) bytes past the end of the packet buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure of heap/stack contents adjacent to the packet buffer; potential denial of service via crash if the read goes into unmapped memory.

state.value: min(sizeof(peer_sdes_buf_), attacker-controlled byte) which can exceed (size - 9)
can be as small as 9 while reason.slen is large

initial_state.value: pointer to incoming RTCP BYE packet (attacker-controlled content)

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Attacker sends a packet of size 10, which is greater than 8

step_by_step_execution.description: reason.slen is set to min(sizeof(peer_sdes_buf_), byte at pkt+8). Attacker sets pkt[8]=200.
pj_memcpy copies reason.slen bytes from pkt+9 into peer_sdes_buf_. Only 1 byte of valid data exists (size-9=1), but up to 200 bytes are read.

out_state.value: contains 1 valid byte + 199 bytes of out-of-bounds data

judgments.reasoning: This finding precisely identifies the CVE-2021-43804 vulnerability: the declared reason length from the packet is not validated against the actual packet size, leading to an out-of-bound read in pj_memcpy. The CWE, impact, and code snippets all match the ground truth.

actual_issue_summary: In parse_rtcp_bye, the reason length byte from the RTCP BYE packet was only clamped to the destination buffer size but not to the actual remaining packet size (size-9), allowing an out-of-bounds read. The fix adds an additional PJ_MIN(reason.slen, size-9) check.

cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4974.

undesired_operation.description: In the source_from_buf path, after ga_concat and appending NUL, ga.ga_len is incremented past the NUL byte. The subsequent STRLEN(buf + ga.ga_len) reads from uninitialized/stale heap memory past the NUL terminator, producing a garbage length. This inflated 'len' is then used to index buf[len - 1], which can read/write out of bounds of the allocated growarray buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer over-read via STRLEN on uninitialized memory, followed by potential out-of-bounds read/write on buf[len-1] and other accesses using the corrupted len value. Could lead to crash (denial of service) or information disclosure.

state.value: points past the NUL terminator after increment
corrupted value from STRLEN reading past NUL into uninitialized heap memory

step_by_step_execution.description: ga_concat appends the buffer line content to ga, updating ga.ga_len to the length of the concatenated string (no NUL counted)

out_state.value: N (length of the line from buflines)

step_by_step_execution.description: NUL byte is appended and ga.ga_len is post-incremented

in_state.value: about to be set to NUL

out_state.value: N+1 (now points one past the NUL)

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: We are in the source_from_buf branch, so after the NUL append we exit the if-block and fall through to the shared len computation

step_by_step_execution.description: len is computed as ga.ga_len + STRLEN(buf + ga.ga_len). Since buf[N] is NUL and ga.ga_len is N+1, STRLEN starts reading from buf[N+1] which is uninitialized/stale heap memory. STRLEN scans until it finds a NUL byte, potentially reading far beyond the valid buffer.

out_state.value: N+1 + (arbitrary value from STRLEN on uninitialized memory), potentially much larger than allocated buffer

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the CVE-2022-1769 vulnerability: in the source_from_buf path, ga.ga_len is incremented past the NUL terminator, causing the shared STRLEN(buf + ga.ga_len) computation to read past the end of valid data. The fix moves len computation into each branch - setting len = ga.ga_len in the buf path (avoiding STRLEN entirely) and keeping STRLEN only in the fgets path.

actual_issue_summary: In get_one_sourceline(), when sourcing from a buffer (source_from_buf path), after appending a NUL byte and incrementing ga.ga_len, the len calculation using STRLEN(buf + ga.ga_len) reads past the NUL terminator into uninitialized heap memory, causing a buffer over-read. The fix computes len separately for each path: len = ga.ga_len for the buffer path, and len = ga.ga_len + STRLEN(...) only for the fgets path.

cve_desc: off-by-one in io_uring module.

undesired_operation.description: Off-by-one error in bounds check: when fd == ctx->nr_user_files, the check 'fd > ctx->nr_user_files' is false, so the code does not return -EBADF. array_index_nospec(fd, ctx->nr_user_files) then clamps fd to 0 (since fd is not strictly less than nr_user_files), causing the code to look up and cancel operations for the file at slot 0 instead of the intended (invalid) slot.
code_snippets:
file_ptr = io_fixed_file_slot(&ctx->file_table, fd)->file_ptr;
cd->file = (struct file *) (file_ptr & FFS_MASK);
undesired_operation.impact: An attacker can pass fd equal to nr_user_files, bypassing the bounds check. array_index_nospec clamps the index to 0, causing cancellation of I/O operations belonging to the file registered at fixed slot 0 rather than returning an error. This allows unauthorized cancellation of another file's pending operations.

initial_state.value: positive integer representing registered file count

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: cd->flags has both IORING_ASYNC_CANCEL_FD and IORING_ASYNC_CANCEL_FD_FIXED set
fd == ctx->nr_user_files, so fd > ctx->nr_user_files is false. The bounds check fails to catch the off-by-one case.

step_by_step_execution.description: array_index_nospec(fd, ctx->nr_user_files) is called with fd == nr_user_files. Since fd is not strictly less than nr_user_files, array_index_nospec clamps the index to 0.
io_fixed_file_slot looks up slot 0 instead of the intended invalid slot, and cd->file is set to the file at slot 0.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the exact CVE-2022-3103 off-by-one vulnerability: using > instead of >= in the bounds check allows fd == ctx->nr_user_files to pass through, matching CWE-193. The impact description about array_index_nospec clamping to 0 and causing wrong file cancellation is a valid consequence.

actual_issue_summary: Off-by-one error in __io_sync_cancel: the bounds check used 'fd > ctx->nr_user_files' instead of 'fd >= ctx->nr_user_files', allowing an fd value equal to nr_user_files to bypass validation.

cve_desc: An out-of-bounds access issue was found in the Linux kernel sound subsystem. It could occur when the 'id->name' provided by the user did not end with '\0'. A privileged local user could pass a specially crafted name through ioctl() interface and crash the system or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

undesired_operation.description: The for-loop condition checks id->name[i] (null terminator check) BEFORE checking i < SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN (bounds check). If id->name is not null-terminated within SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN bytes, when i == SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN, the code reads id->name[SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN], which is one byte past the end of the name array.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read of one byte past the name array. This could leak information from adjacent struct fields into the hash value, or in some memory layouts cause a read from unmapped memory leading to a crash/denial of service.

initial_state.value: user-provided string, possibly not null-terminated within SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop iterates from i=0, each iteration first evaluates id->name[i] before checking bounds

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: When name has no null terminator within the array, all SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN characters are non-zero, so loop continues

When i == SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN, C short-circuit evaluation means id->name[i] is read FIRST, before the bounds check i < SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN can stop iteration. This reads one element past the array.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2022-3170: the out-of-bounds read caused by reversed loop condition checks in get_ctl_id_hash(). The fix reorders the conditions so the bounds check occurs before the array access, which is precisely what the commit does.

actual_issue_summary: In get_ctl_id_hash(), the for-loop condition checked id->name[i] before checking i < SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN. Due to C's left-to-right short-circuit evaluation, if the name array was not null-terminated, the code would read id->name[SNDRV_CTL_ELEM_ID_NAME_MAXLEN] (one past the end) before the bounds check could terminate the loop. The fix reorders the conditions so bounds are checked first.

cve_desc: In drivers/pci/hotplug/rpadlpar_sysfs.c in the Linux kernel through 5.11.8, the RPA PCI Hotplug driver has a user-tolerable buffer overflow when writing a new device name to the driver from userspace, allowing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame directly. This occurs because add_slot_store and remove_slot_store mishandle drc_name '\0' termination, aka CID-cc7a0bb058b8.

undesired_operation.description: strchr is called on drc_name which is not null-terminated after memcpy. strchr scans for '\n' until it finds it or a null byte. Since the bytes beyond the copied region (indices nbytes to MAX_DRC_NAME_LEN-1) are uninitialized stack memory, strchr may read beyond the drc_name buffer if no '\n' or '\0' is present in the uninitialized region, causing a stack out-of-bounds read.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure of stack contents or denial of service via crash if strchr reads past the stack frame boundary.

state.value: first nbytes bytes copied from buf, remaining bytes are uninitialized stack data

initial_state.value: uninitialized stack buffer of MAX_DRC_NAME_LEN bytes
user-provided data, not necessarily null-terminated within nbytes

step_by_step_execution.description: memcpy copies nbytes from buf into drc_name without null-terminating

out_state.value: first nbytes bytes are from buf, remaining bytes are uninitialized stack data

step_by_step_execution.description: strchr searches drc_name for '\n'. Since drc_name is not null-terminated and the uninitialized region may not contain '\n' or '\0', strchr may read beyond the buffer.

in_state.value: not null-terminated, uninitialized data beyond nbytes

out_state.value: may point to an address beyond drc_name if '\n' found in uninitialized stack memory past the buffer

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: If a '\n' byte exists in uninitialized stack memory beyond nbytes, strchr finds it and end points there. This causes *end = '\0' to write a null byte to an arbitrary stack location.

relevant_state.value: points to '\n' byte found in uninitialized stack memory beyond the copied data

step_by_step_execution.description: *end = '\0' writes a null byte to the location end points to, which may be beyond the drc_name buffer on the stack, corrupting stack data.

in_state.value: points to '\n' found in uninitialized stack memory

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core issue: memcpy does not null-terminate drc_name, causing strchr to scan uninitialized stack memory. This is the same vulnerability as CVE-2021-28972, which describes how the missing null termination leads to stack corruption. The finding focuses on the out-of-bounds read aspect while the CVE emphasizes the stack write corruption, but both stem from the same root cause. The finding accurately describes a component of the CVE.

actual_issue_summary:
In add_slot_store (and remove_slot_store), memcpy copies user data into a stack buffer without null-terminating it. The subsequent strchr for '
' scans uninitialized stack memory beyond the copied bytes. If a '
' byte is found in that uninitialized region, writing '\0' there corrupts the kernel stack. The fix uses strscpy (which null-terminates) and only replaces '
' with '\0' when actually found within the properly terminated string.

cve_desc: vim is vulnerable to Heap-based Buffer Overflow

undesired_operation.description: The variable save_so is declared as 'int' but is assigned from '*so_ptr' which is a 'long'. On 64-bit platforms where long is 8 bytes and int is 4 bytes, values of *so_ptr (i.e., curwin->w_p_so or p_so) that exceed INT_MAX will be truncated. At the end of the function, '*so_ptr = save_so' writes back the truncated value, corrupting the scrolloff option.
undesired_operation.impact: Corruption of the scrolloff (so) option value. If a user (or a modeline in a crafted file) sets scrolloff to a value larger than INT_MAX, the value is silently truncated and written back, corrupting the setting. This can also affect scrolling logic within the function since the restored value differs from the original.

initial_state.value: value from curwin->w_p_so or p_so, potentially > INT_MAX

step_by_step_execution.description: save_so is assigned from *so_ptr, truncating from long to int
At function end, *so_ptr is restored from truncated save_so

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE-2021-3903 is about setting VALID_BOTLINE|VALID_BOTLINE_AP in w_valid when the screen is not valid, which leads to a heap-based buffer overflow during scrolling operations. The fix removes this flag setting. The finding describes an unrelated integer truncation issue (long to int) for the save_so variable, which has nothing to do with the actual vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: When there is no valid screen, update_topline() incorrectly set VALID_BOTLINE|VALID_BOTLINE_AP in w_valid. This caused later code to trust w_botline as valid when it wasn't properly computed, leading to invalid memory access (heap-based buffer overflow) during scrolling operations. The fix removes the VALID_BOTLINE flag setting in the no-valid-screen early return path.

cve_desc: Directory traversal vulnerability in the client in HexChat 2.11.0 allows remote IRC servers to read or modify arbitrary files via a .. (dot dot) in the server name.

undesired_operation.description: Repeated strcat calls into a 256-byte stack buffer overflow when the server sends duplicate capability extensions. The extensions_str is attacker-controlled (from IRC server) and split by spaces; each matching extension is appended without checking remaining buffer space or deduplicating.
undesired_operation.impact: Stack buffer overflow leading to potential remote code execution. A malicious IRC server can send a crafted CAP LS response with duplicate extensions to overflow the buffer.

state.value: 256-byte stack buffer with "CAP REQ :" prefix (9 bytes)
attacker-controlled string with repeated capability names

initial_state.value: attacker-controlled string from IRC server CAP LS response

step_by_step_execution.description: buffer is initialized with "CAP REQ :" (9 bytes + null)
extensions_str is split by spaces into extensions array

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Loop iterates over each extension in the attacker-controlled array
Each extension is "znc.in/server-time-iso", matching the comparison

step_by_step_execution.description: strcat appends "znc.in/server-time-iso " (23 bytes) to buffer each iteration without bounds checking. After 11 iterations: 9 + 11*23 = 262 bytes written into 256-byte buffer.

out_state.value: 262 bytes written, overflowing the 256-byte stack buffer

judgments.reasoning: The finding describes a buffer overflow (CWE-120/CWE-121) due to unchecked strcat into a fixed-size buffer. While this is a real bug that was fixed in this diff (strcat replaced with g_strlcat), CVE-2016-2087 is specifically about a directory traversal vulnerability (CWE-22) that allows remote IRC servers to read or modify arbitrary files via '..' in the server name. The buffer overflow finding describes a different vulnerability class than the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2016-2087 is a directory traversal vulnerability (CWE-22) where a malicious IRC server name containing '..' could be used to read or modify arbitrary files on the client. This commit ('Clean up handling CAP LS') appears to be a related cleanup that also addresses buffer overflow risks by switching from strcat to g_strlcat and uses a whitelist approach for supported capabilities, but the core CVE is about path traversal via server names, not buffer overflow in CAP LS handling.

cve_desc: A flaw was found in the way the "flags" member of the new pipe buffer structure was lacking proper initialization in copy_page_to_iter_pipe and push_pipe functions in the Linux kernel and could thus contain stale values. An unprivileged local user could use this flaw to write to pages in the page cache backed by read only files and as such escalate their privileges on the system.

undesired_operation.description: When initializing a new pipe buffer entry with a page cache page, buf->flags is never cleared. If the previous occupant of that pipe buffer slot had PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE set, the flag persists. A subsequent pipe_write() will see CAN_MERGE on a page cache page and write directly into it, allowing modification of read-only file data.
undesired_operation.impact: Privilege escalation — an unprivileged user can overwrite data in read-only file page cache pages, enabling modification of arbitrary read-only files including SUID binaries or /etc/passwd.

state.value: stale value from previous pipe buffer occupant, potentially containing PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE

initial_state.value: uninitialized / stale from previous use of this pipe buffer slot

step_by_step_execution.description: A new pipe_buffer is allocated in push_pipe by indexing into the pipe ring buffer

in_state.value: points to pipe->bufs[iter_head & p_mask], which may have been previously used

out_state.value: stale value from previous occupant, possibly PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE

step_by_step_execution.description: buf->ops, buf->page, buf->offset, buf->len are all initialized, but buf->flags is never set

out_state.value: still stale — no assignment to buf->flags exists in this code path

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: If the previous occupant had CAN_MERGE set and flags was never cleared, this check in pipe_write() will pass, allowing direct writes into the page cache page

judgments.reasoning: This finding precisely describes CVE-2022-0847 (Dirty Pipe): the missing initialization of buf->flags in push_pipe allows stale PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE to persist, enabling writes to read-only page cache pages and privilege escalation. The CWEs (CWE-908 uninitialized resource, CWE-281 improper preservation of permissions) are appropriate characterizations of the flaw. The code snippet shown is from the related copy_page_to_iter_pipe function which has the same bug, making this a valid component of the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2022-0847 (Dirty Pipe): The push_pipe() and copy_page_to_iter_pipe() functions fail to initialize the 'flags' member of newly allocated pipe_buffer structures. Stale PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE flags from previous buffer occupants allow pipe_write() to write directly into page cache pages backed by read-only files, enabling unprivileged privilege escalation.

cve_desc: In drivers/pci/hotplug/rpadlpar_sysfs.c in the Linux kernel through 5.11.8, the RPA PCI Hotplug driver has a user-tolerable buffer overflow when writing a new device name to the driver from userspace, allowing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame directly. This occurs because add_slot_store and remove_slot_store mishandle drc_name '\0' termination, aka CID-cc7a0bb058b8.

undesired_operation.description: strchr is called on drc_name which is not null-terminated after memcpy. If the copied bytes contain no '\n' and no '\0', strchr reads past the copied data into uninitialized stack memory and potentially past the drc_name buffer boundary, as it searches until it finds '\n' or '\0'.
undesired_operation.impact:
Out-of-bounds stack read. strchr scans uninitialized memory beyond the copied bytes looking for '
'. If a '
' is found in uninitialized stack data beyond nbytes but within the buffer, the string passed to dlpar_remove_slot contains uninitialized stack contents (info leak to a privileged operation). If neither '
' nor '\0' is found within the buffer, strchr reads past the stack buffer entirely, potentially causing a crash or further information disclosure.

state.value: not null-terminated after memcpy; bytes beyond nbytes are uninitialized stack data

initial_state.value: uninitialized stack buffer of MAX_DRC_NAME_LEN bytes
user-supplied data, potentially not null-terminated and containing no newline

out_state.value: first nbytes bytes set from buf; remaining bytes are uninitialized stack data

out_state.value: pointer to '\n' found in uninitialized stack data, or NULL if '\0' found first

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: If '\n' is found beyond nbytes in uninitialized stack data, the code falls through to *end = '\0', writing NUL to an arbitrary stack location and including garbage in drc_name

relevant_state.value: points to '\n' byte in uninitialized stack area beyond copied data

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the root cause of CVE-2021-28972: memcpy does not null-terminate drc_name, causing strchr to scan uninitialized stack memory. The CVE description states the issue is that add_slot_store mishandles drc_name '\0' termination, and the fix replaces memcpy with strscpy to ensure null termination. While the finding emphasizes the read aspect (CWE-125/CWE-908) rather than the stack write corruption (CWE-120), it describes the same underlying vulnerability and a direct component/consequence of the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: memcpy copies user data into a stack buffer without null-terminating it. strchr then searches this non-null-terminated buffer for '\n', potentially finding one in uninitialized stack memory beyond the copied bytes. When found, writing '\0' at that position corrupts data on the stack. The fix uses strscpy to ensure null termination and only replaces '\n' with '\0' when actually present in the copied string.

cve_desc: Access of Memory Location Before Start of Buffer in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

undesired_operation.description: When evaluating a '@' register expression in vim9script mode, if the register character is whitespace/NUL or an invalid yank register, an error message is emitted but 'ret' is never set to FAIL, leaving rettv->v_type == VAR_UNKNOWN and continuing execution into handle_subscript() and eval7_leader().
code_snippets:
if (ret == OK)
	ret = handle_subscript(arg, name_start, rettv, evalarg, TRUE);

if (ret == OK && evaluate && end_leader > start_leader)
	ret = eval7_leader(rettv, FALSE, start_leader, &end_leader);
undesired_operation.impact: Use of uninitialized typval_T (v_type == VAR_UNKNOWN) in handle_subscript and eval7_leader can lead to unexpected behavior, potential crashes, or memory corruption when the code attempts to operate on the uninitialized value.

state.value: OK (never set to FAIL after error in '@' case)

step_by_step_execution.description: rettv->v_type is initialized to VAR_UNKNOWN

After incrementing *arg, the next character is whitespace or NUL

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: ret was never changed from OK despite the error

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a potential issue with uninitialized typval when handling '@' register expressions. The actual CVE-2022-0351 is about unbounded recursion in eval7() when processing expressions with many nested parentheses '(', causing a stack overflow crash (access of memory location before start of buffer). The fix adds a recursion limit of 1000. The finding describes a completely different issue.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is an unbounded recursion in eval7() triggered by expressions with many nested parentheses '('. Each '(' causes recursive calls through eval1() back to eval7(), and with enough nesting (e.g., 10000+ levels), the call stack overflows, causing a crash and access of memory before the start of the stack buffer. The fix adds a static recursion counter limited to 1000 levels.

cve_desc: Heap-based buffer overflow in sqbaselib.cpp in SQUIRREL 3.2 due to lack of a certain sq_reservestack call.

undesired_operation.description: sq_call is invoked with nparams on the thread's stack, but only nparams items were pushed. sq_call expects nparams parameters PLUS the closure beneath them (nparams+1 items total from the top). This causes sq_call to read the 'closure' from a position below what was pushed — a stack underflow on the thread VM's stack, potentially reading uninitialized or out-of-bounds memory and treating it as a callable closure object (type confusion).
undesired_operation.impact: An attacker can cause a stack underflow on the thread VM, leading to type confusion where an arbitrary stack value (or out-of-bounds memory) is treated as a closure. This can lead to denial of service (crash) or potentially arbitrary code execution if the attacker can control the memory below the thread's stack.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2022-30292) is a heap-based buffer overflow caused by the missing sq_reservestack call. Without reserving enough space on the thread's (heap-allocated) stack, pushing the roottable and parameters via Push/sq_move overflows the thread's stack buffer. The finding instead describes a stack underflow / type confusion theory about sq_call reading below pushed items, which is an incorrect characterization of the bug. The fix adds sq_reservestack to ensure the thread's stack has enough capacity before items are pushed, preventing heap overflow — not fixing a stack underflow or type confusion.

actual_issue_summary: The thread_call function pushes items (roottable + parameters) onto a thread VM's stack without first ensuring the stack has enough allocated space via sq_reservestack. Since the VM stack is heap-allocated, this causes a heap-based buffer overflow when many parameters are passed. The fix adds sq_reservestack(_thread(o), nparams + 3) before pushing items.

cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to a double free in bobj.c:rz_bin_reloc_storage_free() when freeing relocations generated from qnx binary plugin. A user opening a malicious qnx binary could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. Commit number a3d50c1ea185f3f642f2d8180715f82d98840784 contains a patch for this issue.

undesired_operation.description: Null pointer dereference when accessing qo->fixups if bf->o->bin_obj is NULL
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash — if bin_obj is NULL, dereferencing qo to access fixups causes a segfault

step_by_step_execution.description: bf and bf->o are validated non-NULL by macro
qo is assigned from bf->o->bin_obj which could be NULL
qo->fixups is dereferenced causing NULL pointer dereference

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE-2022-36043 is a double-free (CWE-415). The `rz_list_clone` performs a shallow copy, so both the original fixups list and the returned cloned list share the same RzBinReloc pointers. When both are freed, the reloc objects are freed twice. The fix replaces the shallow clone with deep copies of each reloc. The finding describes a hypothetical NULL pointer dereference which is unrelated to the actual vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: The relocs() function used rz_list_clone() to shallow-copy qo->fixups, meaning both the original list and the returned list contained pointers to the same RzBinReloc objects. When both lists were later freed, each reloc was freed twice (double-free, CWE-415). The fix creates new deep copies of each RzBinReloc instead of shallow-cloning the list.

cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Write in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.

undesired_operation.description: STRCPY writes 7 bytes ("'<,'>+" plus NUL) at orig_cmd + len, resulting in len + 7 total bytes written into a buffer that originally held len + 6 bytes (5 for "'<,'>" prefix + len for the rest + 1 NUL). This is a 1-byte out-of-bounds write.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow by one byte. An attacker who can control the command string in ex mode with a visual range can corrupt heap metadata or adjacent data, potentially leading to code execution or crash.

state.value: len + 5 + 1 (original content was 5-byte prefix + len modifier chars + NUL)
len + 6 + 1 (len modifier chars moved + 6-char suffix "'<,'>+" + NUL)

eap->cmd (points to start of allocated command buffer)

step_by_step_execution.description: The command string starts with "'<,'>", so eap->cmd is advanced by 5 and cmd_start is set

in_state.value: points to "'<,'>mods" (some modifiers after visual range prefix)

step_by_step_execution.description: Modifiers are parsed in the for loop, advancing eap->cmd past them. Then an empty command in ex mode triggers use_plus_cmd = TRUE.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The command started with '<,'> so has_visual_range was set to TRUE
eap->cmd was advanced past modifiers, so it is beyond cmd_start
In ex mode with empty command after modifiers, use_plus_cmd was set to TRUE

step_by_step_execution.description: mch_memmove copies len bytes of modifier text to orig_cmd, then STRCPY appends "'<,'>+" (6 chars + NUL = 7 bytes) at orig_cmd + len. The original buffer was allocated for 5 + len + 1 bytes, but now len + 7 bytes are written, exceeding the buffer by 1 byte.

out_state.value: len + 7 (len bytes of modifiers + 7 bytes for "'<,'>+\0")

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the out-of-bounds write that is the subject of CVE-2022-2288. The original buffer holds "'<,'>" (5 bytes) + modifiers (len bytes) + NUL = 5+len+1 bytes. After mch_memmove moves the modifiers to the start and STRCPY appends "'<,'>+" (6 chars + NUL), the total written is len+7, which is 1 byte beyond the allocated buffer. The fix replaces "'<,'>+" with " *+" (3 chars + NUL) to avoid the buffer growing beyond its allocation. This matches the CVE description of an out-of-bounds write (CWE-787).

actual_issue_summary: In parse_command_modifiers(), when handling a visual range command in Ex mode with modifiers and an empty command, the code replaces the "'<,'>" prefix with the modifier text and appends "'<,'>+". This makes the resulting string 1 byte longer than the original allocated buffer, causing an out-of-bounds write. The fix replaces "'<,'>+" with the shorter " *+" to keep the string within bounds.

cve_desc: PlayerGeneric.cpp in MilkyTracker through 1.02.00 has a use-after-free in the PlayerGeneric destructor.

undesired_operation.description: After 'delete mixer' frees the mixer object, the code dereferences the freed pointer via mixer->isActive() and mixer->isDeviceRemoved(player), resulting in a use-after-free.
undesired_operation.impact: Crash or potential code execution — the freed mixer object's memory may be reused, and calling virtual methods on it can hijack control flow.

pointer to a valid PlayerBase object (or null)

This is exactly the use-after-free in the PlayerGeneric destructor that the fix addresses by reordering the deletion.

actual_issue_summary: In the PlayerGeneric destructor, mixer was deleted before the code that uses mixer->isActive() and mixer->isDeviceRemoved(player). When both mixer and player are non-null, this results in a use-after-free. The fix reorders the destructor to delete mixer after it is no longer needed, and adds a null check for mixer before dereferencing it.

cve_desc: QEMU, through version 2.10 and through version 3.1.0, is vulnerable to an out-of-bounds read of up to 128 bytes in the hw/i2c/i2c-ddc.c:i2c_ddc() function. A local attacker with permission to execute i2c commands could exploit this to read stack memory of the qemu process on the host.

undesired_operation.description: Out-of-bounds read from s->edid_blob[s->reg] when s->reg >= 128. The edid_blob array is 128 bytes but reg can range 0-255, allowing reads up to 128 bytes past the buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure — an attacker can read up to 128 bytes of stack/struct memory beyond the edid_blob buffer by repeatedly invoking I2C read transactions.

initial_state.value: 0-255 (uint8_t, incremented on each call without bounds check)
